title: Florida Small Business Insurance Requirements: What You Need to Know
summary: Learn about Florida's mandatory insurance requirements for small businesses, including BOP, GL, and workers comp, from a trusted agent since 1966.
body:
Florida Small Business Insurance: The Basics

Florida law requires most small businesses to carry specific types of insurance. At a minimum, you'll need general liability (GL) coverage, and if you have employees, workers comp is mandatory. A Business Owner's Policy (BOP) bundles GL with property insurance, often at a discount. Since 1966, Tomlinson & Co has helped Florida businesses stay compliant and protected.

Key Florida Insurance Requirements

  • Workers Compensation: Required for businesses with 4+ employees (including part-time). Construction businesses need it even with 1 employee.
  • Commercial Auto: Mandatory if your business owns vehicles. Learn more about commercial auto requirements.
  • Professional Liability: Often required for licensed professionals like contractors and consultants.

Why Florida Businesses Need Extra Protection

Florida's unique risks—hurricanes, sinkholes, and high litigation rates—mean standard policies may not be enough. Many businesses add flood insurance or umbrella policies. For construction and trades, workers comp rates are particularly high due to Florida's dangerous jobsites.

Call 800-616-1418 or get a free quote online

title: Why Florida Small Businesses Need a Business Owner Policy (BOP)
summary: Discover why Florida small businesses benefit from a Business Owner Policy (BOP), combining property, liability, and business interruption coverage into one affordable package.
body:
Running a small business in Florida comes with unique challenges, from hurricanes to unpredictable weather. A Business Owner Policy (BOP) is a smart, cost-effective solution that bundles essential coverages into one package. At Tomlinson & Co, we’ve helped Florida businesses since 1966 protect their assets and operations with tailored BOPs.

What’s Included in a Florida BOP?

A BOP typically combines three key coverages: property insurance, general liability, and business interruption insurance. In Florida, where hurricanes and tropical storms are a constant threat, property coverage is critical. It protects your building, equipment, and inventory from damage caused by wind, rain, or other disasters. General liability safeguards your business from lawsuits, while business interruption insurance helps cover lost income if you’re forced to close temporarily.

Florida-Specific Risks & Stats

Florida ranks as one of the most hurricane-prone states in the U.S., with an average of 1.63 hurricanes making landfall annually. Small businesses often bear the brunt of these storms, facing costly repairs and downtime. A BOP ensures you’re prepared for these risks. Additionally, Florida’s booming tourism industry means higher foot traffic, increasing the likelihood of liability claims. A BOP provides peace of mind in these scenarios.

Why Choose Tomlinson & Co?

With decades of experience, we understand Florida’s unique insurance needs. Whether you need a BOP, commercial auto, or workers comp, we offer personalized solutions to fit your business. Protect your livelihood with a policy designed for Florida’s challenges.

Call 800-616-1418 or get a free quote online today to secure your Florida small business with a comprehensive BOP.

title: Florida Small Business Insurance Requirements: What You Need to Know
summary: Understand essential Florida small business insurance requirements to protect your business from common risks and legal obligations.
body:
As a licensed agent serving Florida businesses since 1966, I’ve seen firsthand how crucial proper insurance is for success in the Sunshine State. Florida’s unique business landscape, from bustling tourism to growing tech sectors, comes with its own set of risks and, consequently, specific insurance requirements. Let's delve into what your small business needs to stay protected and compliant.

Mandatory Florida Business Insurance

While the specifics can vary based on your industry and employee count, two types of insurance are frequently mandatory for Florida businesses:

  • Workers' Compensation Insurance: If you have four or more employees (full-time or part-time), Florida law generally requires you to carry workers' compensation insurance. For businesses in the construction industry, this threshold drops to just one employee. This coverage protects both your employees and your business in case of work-related injuries or illnesses.
  • Commercial Auto Insurance: If your business uses vehicles for operations – whether it's a delivery van, a service truck, or even an employee using their personal car for business purposes – you'll likely need commercial auto insurance. Personal auto policies typically don't cover business-related incidents, leaving a significant gap in your protection.

Essential Coverage for Florida Small Businesses

Beyond the mandatory policies, wise business owners secure additional coverage to mitigate common Florida risks:

  • General Liability Insurance (GL): This is your foundational protection against claims of bodily injury or property damage that occur on your business premises or due to your operations. Think slips and falls, or accidental damage to a client's property.
  • Business Owner's Policy (BOP): A BOP is a fantastic option for many small businesses. It bundles General Liability, Commercial Property, and often Business Interruption insurance into one convenient and cost-effective package. This protects your physical assets (like your building and equipment) and helps cover lost income if you have to temporarily close due to a covered peril, such as a hurricane.

Florida businesses face unique challenges, from severe weather to high litigation rates. Having the right insurance isn't just a legal obligation; it's a strategic decision that safeguards your investment and peace of mind. Partner with a local expert like Tomlinson & Co to ensure your coverage is tailored to your specific needs.

Don't leave your Florida small business exposed. Call 800-616-1418 or get a free quote online today!

title: Florida Business Owner Policy (BOP): Your All-in-One Small Business Shield
summary: Protect your Florida small business with a comprehensive Business Owner Policy (BOP). Get liability, property, and business interruption coverage tailored for the Sunshine State.
body:
As a licensed agent serving Florida businesses since 1966, I've seen firsthand the unique challenges and opportunities our Sunshine State entrepreneurs face. One of the smartest investments any small business can make is a Business Owner Policy, or BOP. It's not just insurance; it's peace of mind wrapped up in one convenient package.

What is a Florida BOP and Why Do You Need It?

A Florida Business Owner Policy is a comprehensive insurance solution designed specifically for small to medium-sized businesses. Think of it as a bundled deal, combining three essential coverages:

  • Commercial Property Insurance: Protects your building (if you own it), equipment, inventory, and other business assets from perils like fire, theft, and wind damage – a crucial consideration in hurricane-prone Florida. Did you know Florida experiences the highest number of thunderstorms in the US, with an average of 70-100 days of thunderstorms annually?
  • General Liability Insurance: Covers claims of bodily injury or property damage to others caused by your business operations, products, or services. This is vital for any customer-facing business.
  • Business Interruption Insurance (also known as Business Income): Replaces lost income and covers extra expenses if your business has to temporarily close due to a covered loss, like hurricane damage. This can be a lifesaver for businesses in Florida's dynamic economy.

Tailored Protection for Florida's Unique Risks

Florida's vibrant economy and unique climate mean businesses here face specific risks. A BOP through easycommercialinsurance.com can be customized to address these. For instance, while a standard BOP covers many common perils, you might need to consider endorsements for flood insurance or enhanced windstorm coverage depending on your location. And if you have employees, remember that workers comp is a separate, mandatory coverage.

Is a BOP Right for Your Florida Business?

Most small businesses, from retail shops and restaurants to professional services and contractors, can benefit greatly from a BOP. It simplifies your insurance, often at a more affordable premium than purchasing each policy separately. It's about protecting your livelihood so you can focus on what you do best – running your business.

Don't leave your Florida small business exposed. Get the comprehensive protection you need today. Call 800-616-1418 or get a free quote online.

title: What Is a Business Owner's Policy (BOP) and Does Your Florida Business Need One?
summary: A Business Owner's Policy bundles general liability and commercial property coverage into one affordable package. Learn what a BOP covers, what it doesn't, and whether it's the right fit for your Florida business.
body:
If you run a small or mid-sized business in Florida, you've probably heard the term Business Owner's Policy — or BOP. But what exactly is it, and does your business actually need one?

What Is a BOP?

A Business Owner's Policy is a bundled insurance package designed specifically for small and medium-sized businesses. It combines two essential coverages into one policy — and typically at a lower combined premium than buying them separately:

  • General Liability Insurance: Covers third-party bodily injury, property damage, and personal injury claims (like a customer slipping and falling in your store).
  • Commercial Property Insurance: Covers your business building, equipment, inventory, and furniture against damage from fire, theft, vandalism, and certain weather events.

Many BOPs also include business interruption insurance, which pays your lost income and ongoing expenses if a covered event forces you to temporarily shut down.

Who Qualifies for a BOP?

BOPs are designed for small to mid-sized businesses that meet certain criteria. Insurers typically look at:

  • Business size (annual revenue under $5–10 million)
  • Industry type (lower-risk industries qualify more easily)
  • Physical location and square footage
  • Number of employees

High-risk operations — like contractors, manufacturers, or businesses with large fleets — may need to build a commercial package from individual policies rather than buying a BOP.

What a BOP Doesn't Cover

A BOP is a great starting point, but it's not a complete commercial insurance program. Common gaps include:

  • Workers' compensation (required separately in Florida if you have employees — see Florida workers comp requirements)
  • Commercial auto insurance (vehicles used for business need their own policy)
  • Professional liability / errors and omissions
  • Flood damage (just like homeowners policies, BOPs exclude flood)
  • Cyber liability

Is a BOP Right for Your Florida Business?

If you own a retail shop, restaurant, office-based business, or service company with a physical location, a BOP is almost certainly the most cost-effective way to build your foundational coverage. For most Florida small businesses, it's the natural starting point.

Want to know if your business qualifies — and what a BOP would cost? Call the team at EasyCommercialInsurance.com at 800-616-1418. We shop multiple A-rated carriers to find you the best coverage at the best price.

title: General Liability Insurance: The Foundation Every Florida Small Business Needs
summary: General liability insurance is the single most important coverage a Florida small business can carry. Here's what it covers, how much it costs, and why going without it is a serious risk.
body:
Ask any commercial insurance agent what the most essential coverage is for a Florida small business, and the answer is almost always the same: general liability insurance. It's the foundation that everything else is built on — and without it, a single lawsuit could wipe out everything you've built.

What Does General Liability Cover?

General liability (GL) insurance protects your business against claims from third parties — customers, vendors, or members of the public. Key coverages include:

  • Bodily injury: A customer slips on a wet floor in your store and breaks their wrist. Your GL covers their medical bills and any lawsuit that follows.
  • Property damage: Your employee accidentally damages a client's equipment while on a job. GL covers the repair or replacement.
  • Personal and advertising injury: Someone claims your ad campaign copied their idea or damaged their reputation. GL can cover the legal defense and settlement.
  • Medical payments: Covers minor injuries to third parties regardless of fault — often resolving small incidents before they become lawsuits.

What General Liability Does NOT Cover

General liability has important exclusions Florida business owners should understand:

  • Your own employees' injuries (that's workers' compensation)
  • Your own property damage (that's commercial property insurance)
  • Professional mistakes or advice errors (that's professional liability / E&O)
  • Auto accidents involving business vehicles (that's commercial auto)
  • Intentional acts

How Much Does It Cost?

For most Florida small businesses, general liability insurance runs $400–$1,500 per year for a $1 million per occurrence / $2 million aggregate policy. The biggest factors affecting price are:

  • Industry (a staffing agency pays far more than a graphic designer)
  • Annual revenue
  • Number of employees
  • Claims history

Who Requires It?

Many Florida business situations require proof of general liability coverage:

  • Commercial lease agreements — most landlords require it
  • Vendor contracts and client agreements
  • Government contracts and business licenses
  • Trade shows and events

Even when it's not required, going without GL is a risk that no serious Florida business owner should take. Call EasyCommercialInsurance.com at 800-616-1418 — we'll find you the best general liability rate in minutes.

title: Workers' Compensation in Florida: What Employers Are Required to Carry
summary: Florida workers' compensation laws are strict — and the penalties for non-compliance are severe. Here's a plain-English breakdown of what Florida employers are legally required to carry.
body:
Florida has some of the strictest workers' compensation laws in the country. If you employ workers in the state — even part-time — you need to understand your legal obligations. Non-compliance doesn't just mean fines. It can mean a stop-work order that shuts down your business on the spot.

Who Is Required to Carry Workers' Comp in Florida?

Florida law bases the requirement on industry type:

  • Construction industry: Any employer with one or more employees (including the owner) must carry workers' compensation. This is the strictest standard in the country.
  • Non-construction industries: Employers with four or more employees are required to carry coverage.
  • Agricultural employers: Required to cover employees if they have six or more regular employees, or 12 or more seasonal workers who work more than 30 days during the season.

Corporate officers and LLC members in construction are included in the employee count unless they file for an exemption with the Florida Division of Workers' Compensation.

What Workers' Comp Covers

Workers' compensation covers your employees if they're injured on the job — regardless of who was at fault. Key benefits include:

  • Medical treatment: 100% of work-related medical expenses with no deductible to the employee
  • Lost wages: Temporary partial or total disability benefits (typically 66⅔% of the employee's average weekly wage)
  • Permanent disability benefits: For lasting injuries that affect earning capacity
  • Death benefits: For families of workers killed on the job

In exchange for this coverage, employees generally cannot sue their employer directly for workplace injuries — this is the core trade-off known as the exclusive remedy doctrine.

Consequences of Non-Compliance

Florida's Department of Financial Services conducts random compliance audits. If you're found operating without required coverage:

  • Immediate stop-work order — your business shuts down on the spot
  • Penalty equal to 2x the premium you would have paid for the prior two years
  • Possible criminal charges for continued non-compliance

How Much Does Workers' Comp Cost in Florida?

Florida workers' compensation rates are class-code based, tied to the specific type of work your employees do. Rates are expressed per $100 of payroll. A roofing contractor pays dramatically more than a software company. An independent agent can run multiple carrier quotes to find the most competitive rate for your payroll class.

Need workers' comp for your Florida business? Visit our dedicated Florida workers comp resource or call EasyCommercialInsurance.com at 800-616-1418 — we'll get you compliant quickly.

title: Commercial Property Insurance vs. Homeowners Insurance for Home-Based Businesses
summary: Running a business from home? Your homeowners policy almost certainly doesn't cover your business property or liability. Here's what home-based Florida business owners need to know.
body:
Florida has hundreds of thousands of home-based businesses — consultants, contractors, photographers, e-commerce sellers, tutors, and more. If you're one of them, there's a critical insurance gap you may not know about: your homeowners policy almost certainly doesn't cover your business activities.

What Your Homeowners Policy Covers (and Doesn't)

A standard Florida homeowners policy is designed to protect your personal residence and belongings — not a commercial operation. Here are the typical limits that catch home-based business owners off guard:

  • Business property cap: Most homeowners policies cap coverage for business equipment (computers, inventory, tools) at just $2,500. If you have $20,000 in equipment, you're largely unprotected.
  • No business liability: If a client or vendor visits your home and gets injured, or if you cause property damage in the course of doing business, your homeowners liability won't respond — it specifically excludes business activities.
  • No business interruption: If a covered loss forces you out of your home, your homeowners policy won't replace your lost business income.
  • Professional services excluded: Any claims arising from advice or services you provide are not covered under a personal homeowners policy.

Your Options as a Home-Based Business Owner

Depending on the size and type of your operation, you have several paths to close these gaps:

  • Home Business Endorsement: Some insurers offer a home-based business rider that expands your existing homeowners coverage. Best for very small, low-risk operations with limited equipment.
  • In-Home Business Policy: A standalone policy designed for home-based businesses. Covers business property, liability, and sometimes business interruption. More comprehensive than an endorsement.
  • Business Owner's Policy (BOP): For growing home-based businesses, a BOP may actually be the best option — it provides the full commercial property and liability coverage your operation needs at a competitive price, even if you work from home.

When to Have the Conversation

The time to address this isn't after a claim is denied. If you have clients coming to your home, store inventory, use expensive equipment for your business, or provide professional services from your residence — talk to a commercial insurance agent today. Home-based businesses in Florida can also explore whether a Florida homeowners insurance endorsement could close some gaps, though a standalone commercial policy is usually the better fit.

The team at EasyCommercialInsurance.com can review your situation and recommend the right combination of coverage. Call us at 800-616-1418 — it's a free consultation.

title: Top 5 Insurance Mistakes Florida Small Business Owners Make
summary: Most Florida small business owners are underinsured, over-paying, or both. Here are the five most common commercial insurance mistakes — and how to avoid them.
body:
After years of helping Florida small business owners get properly insured, we've seen the same costly mistakes repeat themselves over and over. Some result in massive out-of-pocket losses. Others mean paying far too much for coverage that doesn't fit. Here are the top five — and how to avoid them.

1. Skipping General Liability Because 'Nothing Bad Has Happened Yet'

This is the most common and most dangerous mistake. Business owners operate for years without a significant incident and conclude they don't need liability coverage. Then a customer trips over a display, a contractor accidentally floods a client's office, or an advertising dispute lands them in court — and suddenly they're facing a five- or six-figure liability with no coverage. General liability is one of the most affordable policies a small business can carry. The risk of going without it almost never makes financial sense.

2. Not Reviewing Coverage Limits After the Business Grows

Many business owners buy coverage when they launch — and never update it. But if your revenue has doubled, you've hired employees, added equipment, or moved to a larger space, your original policy limits may be woefully inadequate. Insurance-to-value matters. If you're underinsured and have a major property loss, you may only collect a fraction of the actual damage. Review your coverage annually.

3. Assuming Homeowners Insurance Covers Home-Based Business Activity

We covered this in depth in our previous post — but it's worth repeating because it catches so many Florida business owners off guard. Standard homeowners policies strictly limit business property coverage and exclude business liability entirely. If you run any part of your business from your home, you need dedicated commercial coverage.

4. Buying the Cheapest Policy Without Comparing Coverage

Price comparison is smart. Price-only comparison is dangerous. Two general liability policies priced $400 apart can have dramatically different exclusions, sublimits, and claims handling reputations. Always compare coverage terms — not just premium. An independent agent who represents multiple carriers can show you what you're actually getting for your money across each option.

5. Not Working with a Commercial Insurance Specialist

Commercial insurance is meaningfully different from personal lines. A personal lines agent who occasionally writes a BOP is not the same as a specialist who handles commercial accounts daily. Industry-specific knowledge matters — whether you're a restaurant, a contractor, a medical practice, or a tech company, the coverage nuances are real, and getting it wrong costs money.

The EasyCommercialInsurance.com team focuses exclusively on Florida business coverage. We know the carriers, the classes, and the coverage gaps. Call us at 800-616-1418 — a 15-minute conversation could save you thousands and eliminate a lot of risk.
